summary: The RMAC system has a coherent end-to-end pipeline (discover → resolve → discuss → eval) and reliable infrastructure (Vertex backend, tectonic compilation, structured proof storage), but it is bottlenecked by a single-model monoculture and a complete absence of formal verification — 0/10 proofs are verified despite 10 having 'best' proofs. The most urgent strategic gap is that solvability is measured by a self-referential LLM scorer rather than ground truth, creating a high risk of the cluster optimizing for self-agreement rather than correctness. Resource usage is near-idle (10 total runs, $3.78 cumulative), so the system is under-utilized and has ample headroom to add diversity, verification, and adversarial checks.
problems: Zero verified proofs (0/10): the pipeline produces 'best proofs' and 0-100% solvability scores with no formal/external ground-truth check, so high scores cannot be trusted.

Single-model monoculture: every proof, critic, solver, and evaluator runs on one model family. Shared blind spots mean the critic and solver fail to catch the same errors, and the evaluator rubber-stamps them.
Self-referential evaluation loop: the solvability-evaluator is an LLM scoring proofs written by sibling LLMs with no independent reference, inviting score inflation and reward-hacking.
Meeting/discussion subsystem is completely unused (0 rooms, 0 messages) despite being a defined pipeline stage — either dead weight or a missing automation trigger.

Low overall issue resolution rate (26%) with 391 open and only 17 in-progress suggests the solver-agent stalls or issues are opened faster than they can be closed; no triage/prioritization is evident.
Under-utilization of compute: 0 runs today and only 10 total runs means the daily push-forward is barely exercising the system, leaving little signal to debug agent behavior.
Observability gap: no per-agent success metrics, no record of why an issue stays open, and no diff between proof versions surfaced — engineers cannot tell when an agent silently degrades.
highlights: Clean, well-separated agent architecture with distinct responsibilities (critic, solver, meeting personas, evaluator, meta-analyst) and a logical pipeline ordering.
Robust reproducible infrastructure: local tectonic LaTeX compilation, structured best-proof JSON plus versioned .tex storage, and ADC-based Vertex access.
Daily push-forward automation already chains all pipeline steps across all problems, giving a foundation to layer verification and diversity onto.
Issue-based decomposition (274 problems → 551 granular issues) is a sound strategy for making large proofs tractable and measurable.
Cost discipline is excellent — cumulative spend is trivial ($3.78), leaving budget to invest in multi-model ensembling and adversarial verification.

suggested_todos.title: Add a formal verification / external ground-truth stage (e.g. proof-assistant or independent referee model) so 'verified' is a real gate, not just a score
Introduce model diversity: run solver and evaluator on different model families than the proof generator to break shared blind spots
Make the solvability-evaluator adversarial and reference-anchored — require it to cite the specific gap it could NOT close rather than emit a single aggregate score
Instrument per-agent observability: success rate, issue open→resolved latency, score-vs-verification correlation, and proof-version diffs
Add issue triage/prioritization so the solver attacks high-impact open issues first instead of letting 391 accumulate
Either activate the meeting subsystem via the push-forward trigger or remove it until it produces measurable proof improvements
Add a regression guard that blocks a new 'best proof' from replacing the prior one unless its score improvement is independently confirmed
Run multiple independent solve attempts per issue and ensemble/vote rather than relying on a single chain

mistakes: Treating an LLM-generated solvability score as a proxy for correctness without any external verification — the system can score itself well while being wrong.
Using the same model for generation, critique, and evaluation, which collapses the intended checks-and-balances into a single correlated point of failure.
Defining pipeline stages (meetings) that never actually run, so reported architecture overstates real capability.
Opening issues far faster than they are resolved, letting the open backlog balloon while in-progress work stays near zero — discovery without throughput.
Equating 'has a best proof' with progress: 10/10 problems have a best proof but 0/10 are verified, conflating output volume with output quality.
Leaving the cluster idle (0 runs today) means failures and regressions go undetected because the system isn't generating enough signal to monitor.
